title: Fuel and emission control structure description - Fuel injection control

blocks.text: Fuel and emission control structure description - Fuel injection control
Fuel injection control uses PGM-FI (Programmed-Fuel Injection: electronically controlled fuel injection) to enable precise fuel control, resulting in higher engine output and lower fuel consumption.

The PGM-FI ECU uses the mass flow method to determine the basic injection amount based on the intake air volume calculated from the air flow meter output, and the PGM-FI ECU uses this basic injection amount as the basis for determining the optimal fuel injection amount for each situation by adding corrections based on each sensor signal. Since the fuel injectors operate with a fixed stroke, the PGM-FI ECU controls the fuel injection amount by the length of time the injectors are energized.

The PGM-FI ECU calculates the injection timing for each cylinder based on the signals from the crank sensor and TDC sensor, and controls the timing of fuel injectors. The injection system uses a sequential injection system.
The crank sensor is mounted on the oil pan and outputs a pulse signal each time the 58 projections of the pulsar rotor mounted on the crankshaft pass by.
The TDC sensor is mounted on the cylinder head and outputs a pulse signal each time the three projections of the pulsar rotor mounted on the camshaft pass by.
A twin-port injection system is used: two fuel injectors per cylinder are mounted on the cylinder head to inject fuel simultaneously.
The twin-port injection system improves fuel economy by improving combustion through atomization of fuel spray and uniformity of mixture distribution.
Air flow meter/intake air temperature sensor1
An air flow meter/intake air temperature sensor1 is installed at the air cleaner outlet to detect the intake air volume and intake air temperature.

The air flow meter is equipped with a hot wire, a cold film, and an IC, and uses the principle that a hot wire is cooled by the intake air flow to change its resistance value to detect the intake air volume.

The control circuit inside the IC detects the resistance values of the hot wire and cold film and controls the current flowing to the hot wire so that the temperature difference between the hot wire and cold film remains constant. The amount of air intake is calculated from this current value, converted to voltage, and output to the PGM-FI ECU.
The intake air temperature sensor 1 uses a thermistor and outputs the resistance value of the thermistor, which changes with the intake air temperature, to the PGM-FI ECU.
